title: Designing Component Trees

learningObjectives.text: Explain splitting a ui into components using a new example and justify the result.
Explain component boundaries using a new example and justify the result.
Explain component categories using a new example and justify the result.

whyThisMatters: Designing Component Trees is part of the mental model learners need before making reliable implementation decisions. It belongs to the **Component Design and Reusability** module.
mentalModel: Draw a decision map: **Splitting a UI into components** → **Component boundaries** → **Component categories**. Add branches where the learner must choose an alternative and label the evidence for the choice.

sections.title: Splitting a UI into components
sections.body: Split where a region has a coherent responsibility, repeated pattern, independent state, or meaningful data boundary.

sections.title: Component boundaries
sections.body: A component boundary defines ownership of markup, behavior, and dependencies. Good boundaries minimize reasons to change.

sections.title: Component categories
sections.body: Page, layout, feature, and reusable UI categories can clarify ownership and dependency direction, but they are team conventions rather than React rules.

sections.title: Worked example
sections.explanation: The page is split along distinct responsibilities.

commonMistakes: Mutating props or state instead of producing the next value.
Adding state or an Effect for a value that can be derived during render.
Using global state before identifying the real owner and consumers.
Optimizing renders without a measured user-facing problem.
summary: Designing Component Trees connects Splitting a UI into components, Component boundaries, Component categories.
Use the topic to make behavior and decisions explicit rather than memorizing isolated syntax.
Verify understanding by explaining a new example and answering the topic quiz without looking back.

topicQuiz.failurePolicy: Show explanations, add missed objectives to local review, and allow immediate or later retry.

choices.label: The page is split along distinct responsibilities.

questions.prompt: What should the developer do before treating this artifact as complete?

choices.label: Compare it with the actual requirement, test a representative success and failure path, and record any assumption that affects later work.
choices.feedback: Workflow artifacts are useful only when they are checked against the real requirement and observable failure paths.

choices.label: Add abstractions until no one can describe the original requirement.
choices.feedback: This choice does not resolve the assessed issue. Re-trace the Designing Component Trees rule and the observable result.

choices.label: Avoid running or reviewing it because the syntax already looks familiar.
choices.feedback: This choice does not resolve the assessed issue. Re-trace the Designing Component Trees rule and the observable result.

choices.label: Replace the artifact with a screenshot so behavior cannot change.
choices.feedback: This choice does not resolve the assessed issue. Re-trace the Designing Component Trees rule and the observable result.

questions.explanation: Workflow artifacts are useful only when they are checked against the real requirement and observable failure paths.
questions.hint: Choose the step that exposes incorrect assumptions before more work depends on them.

questions.prompt: A developer notices this recurring mistake: “Mutating props or state instead of producing the next value.” What is the best response?

choices.label: Return to the requirement, use the Designing Component Trees model to identify the failed assumption, and verify the corrected behavior with a representative case.
choices.feedback: The useful response corrects both the misconception and the next development action for Designing Component Trees.

choices.label: Hide the symptom and continue so the schedule is not affected.
choices.feedback: This choice does not resolve the assessed issue. Re-trace the Designing Component Trees rule and the observable result.

choices.label: Add unrelated abstractions before reproducing the problem.
choices.feedback: This choice does not resolve the assessed issue. Re-trace the Designing Component Trees rule and the observable result.

choices.label: Treat the mistake as unavoidable and remove the relevant check.
choices.feedback: This choice does not resolve the assessed issue. Re-trace the Designing Component Trees rule and the observable result.

questions.explanation: The useful response corrects both the misconception and the next development action for Designing Component Trees.
questions.hint: Choose the response that makes the failure reproducible and the correction verifiable.

questions.prompt: Which day-to-day workflow is most reliable when a task depends on Designing Component Trees?

choices.label: Clarify the requirement, identify the relevant model or boundary, test the smallest representative case, then refactor after the behavior is understood.
choices.feedback: A reliable workflow makes assumptions and failures visible early, then improves the implementation after correctness is established.

choices.label: Start with the largest available abstraction and postpone defining expected behavior.
choices.feedback: This choice does not resolve the assessed issue. Re-trace the Designing Component Trees rule and the observable result.

choices.label: Copy a solution from another project without checking whether its assumptions match.
choices.feedback: This choice does not resolve the assessed issue. Re-trace the Designing Component Trees rule and the observable result.

choices.label: Optimize for line count first and inspect correctness only after release.
choices.feedback: This choice does not resolve the assessed issue. Re-trace the Designing Component Trees rule and the observable result.

questions.explanation: A reliable workflow makes assumptions and failures visible early, then improves the implementation after correctness is established.
questions.hint: Prefer the sequence that produces evidence early and keeps decisions reversible.

questions.prompt: Which guideline shows the best engineering judgment about Designing Component Trees?

choices.label: Use Designing Component Trees to reduce uncertainty: document the requirement, choose a proportionate approach, and leave evidence that another developer can review.
choices.feedback: Use Designing Component Trees to reduce uncertainty: document the requirement, choose a proportionate approach, and leave evidence that another developer can review.

choices.label: Use Designing Component Trees in every file because more abstraction always improves software.
choices.feedback: This choice does not resolve the assessed issue. Re-trace the Designing Component Trees rule and the observable result.

choices.label: Avoid Designing Component Trees completely because every additional concept reduces maintainability.
choices.feedback: This choice does not resolve the assessed issue. Re-trace the Designing Component Trees rule and the observable result.

choices.label: Choose only by the number of lines and ignore correctness, ownership, and future change.
choices.feedback: This choice does not resolve the assessed issue. Re-trace the Designing Component Trees rule and the observable result.

questions.explanation: Use Designing Component Trees to reduce uncertainty: document the requirement, choose a proportionate approach, and leave evidence that another developer can review.
questions.hint: Choose the option that balances correctness, clarity, cost, and the actual requirement.

assessmentProfile.label: Developer workflow and decision scenarios
assessmentProfile.rationale: The learner should make realistic development decisions using requirements, logs, file structures, configuration, or short code artifacts.
assessmentProfile.inLessonPolicy: Use concise concept checks; coding and hybrid topics include at least one code-reading check.
assessmentProfile.topicQuizPolicy: Questions Q04–Q08 form the topic quiz. The presentation mix follows this profile.
